The list of files is truncated. Use listFiles tool to explore if needed)\n\n# GIT STATUS\nCurrent branch: \nMain branch (you will usually use this for PRs): \n\nRecent commits:\n\n\n# TODOs\nNo TODOs yet, if you are working on tasks that would benefit from a todo list please use the todoWrite tool to create one.</system-reminder>"},{"type":"text","text":"# Track Branches with Sparse Checkout Patterns\n\nYou are a platform engineer responsible for maintaining a large monorepo called `platform-mono`. The repository contains multiple services and infrastructure components organized across several top-level directories:\n\n```\nplatform-mono/\n  services/\n    auth/\n    payments/\n    notifications/\n  infra/\n    terraform/\n    kubernetes/\n  docs/\n  shared/\n    libs/\n    protos/\n```\n\nYou primarily work on the `infra` and `shared` components. To keep your working copy lean and avoid noise from unrelated service directories, you want to use sparse checkout so that only the directories relevant to your work are materialized on disk.\n\nYour repository already has some history with commits on several bookmarks (branches). Your task involves setting up sparse checkout, tracking the right remote bookmarks, and verifying the state of the repository.\n\n## Your Tasks\n\n### 1. Set Up Sparse Checkout for Your Work Area\n\nNavigate to the repository at `/home/user/platform-mono`. Configure sparse checkout so that your working copy only contains the following directory trees:\n\n- `infra/`\n- `shared/`\n- `docs/`\n\nUse `jj sparse set` to define the sparse patterns. After setting up sparse checkout, verify the patterns are active using `jj sparse list`.\n\n### 2. Create and Track a Feature Bookmark\n\nYou need to create a new bookmark called `infra/terraform-refactor` that tracks the remote bookmark `origin/infra/terraform-refactor`. This remote bookmark already exists in the repository.\n\nCreate the bookmark at the current working-copy parent revision and configure it to track the corresponding remote bookmark:\n\n```\njj bookmark create infra/terraform-refactor --revision @-\njj bookmark track infra/terraform-refactor@origin\n```\n\nAfter this, confirm the bookmark appears in `jj bookmark list` output.\n\n### 3. Fetch and Reconcile Remote State\n\nFetch updates from the remote to ensure you have the latest state of all tracked bookmarks:\n\n```\njj git fetch\n```\n\nAfter fetching, check `jj bookmark list` to see the current state of all bookmarks and their remote tracking status.\n\n### 4. Update Sparse Patterns to Add a New Service\n\nYour team has informed you that you now also need visibility into the `services/notifications/` directory as it has a dependency on shared protos. Update your sparse checkout patterns to add this directory:\n\n- `infra/`\n- `shared/`\n- `docs/`\n- `services/notifications/`\n\nUse `jj sparse set` again with the updated list of patterns.\n\n### 5. Create a New Commit in the Infra Directory\n\nCreate a new empty commit on top of the current working copy parent using `jj new`. Then create a file at `infra/terraform/backend.tf` with the following content:\n\n```hcl\nterraform {\n  backend \"s3\" {\n    bucket = \"platform-tf-state\"\n    key    = \"infra/terraform.tfstate\"\n    region = \"us-west-2\"\n  }\n}\n```\n\nDescribe this commit with the message: `infra: add terraform s3 backend configuration`\n\n### 6. Verify the Final State\n\nVerify the following conditions are true:\n\n- `jj sparse list` includes `infra/`, `shared/`, `docs/`, and `services/notifications/`\n- `jj bookmark list` shows `infra/terraform-refactor` tracking `infra/terraform-refactor@origin`\n- The file `infra/terraform/backend.tf` exists in the working copy with the correct content\n- `jj log -r 'description(\"infra: add terraform s3 backend configuration\")'` returns a non-empty result\n- `jj status` exits with code 0\n\n## Notes\n\n- Work entirely within `/home/user/platform-mono`\n- Do not use `sudo` or modify system files\n- All `jj` commands should be run from within the repository directory\n- The sparse checkout patterns must use the exact directory names listed above\n- Use `jj describe -m \"...\"` to set commit messages"}]}

I'm now looking at the 'jj sparse set' command and its help information.\n\n\n","state":"done"},{"type":"tool-executeCommand","toolCallId":"TBaivhL0ACSMIbFq","state":"output-available","input":{"command":"cd /home/user/platform-mono && jj help sparse set"},"output":{"output":"Update the patterns that are present in the working copy\r\n\r\nFor example, if all you need is the `README.md` and the `lib/` directory, use `jj sparse set --clear\r\n--add README.md --add lib`. If you no longer need the `lib` directory, use `jj sparse set --remove\r\nlib`.\r\n\r\nUsage: jj sparse set [OPTIONS]\r\n\r\nOptions:\r\n      --add <ADD>\r\n          Patterns to add to the working copy\r\n\r\n      --remove <REMOVE>\r\n          Patterns to remove from the working copy\r\n\r\n      --clear\r\n          Include no files in the working copy (combine with --add)\r\n\r\n  -h, --help\r\n          Print help (see a summary with '-h')\r\n\r\nGlobal Options:\r\n  -R, --repository <REPOSITORY>\r\n          Path to repository to operate on\r\n          \r\n          By default, Jujutsu searches for the closest .jj/ directory in an ancestor of the current\r\n          working directory.\r\n\r\n      --ignore-working-copy\r\n          Don't snapshot the working copy, and don't update it\r\n          \r\n          By default, Jujutsu snapshots the working copy at the beginning of every command. The\r\n          working copy is also updated at the end of the command, if the command modified the\r\n          working-copy commit (`@`). If you want to avoid snapshotting the working copy and instead\r\n          see a possibly stale working-copy commit, you can use `--ignore-working-copy`. This may be\r\n          useful e.g. in a command prompt, especially if you have another process that commits the\r\n          working copy.\r\n          \r\n          Loading the repository at a specific operation with `--at-operation` implies\r\n          `--ignore-working-copy`.\r\n\r\n      --ignore-immutable\r\n          Allow rewriting immutable commits\r\n          \r\n          By default, Jujutsu prevents rewriting commits in the configured set of immutable commits.\r\n          This option disables that check and lets you rewrite any commit but the root commit.\r\n          \r\n          This option only affects the check. It does not affect the `immutable_heads()` revset or\r\n          the `immutable` template keyword.\r\n\r\n      --at-operation <AT_OPERATION>\r\n          Operation to load the repo at\r\n          \r\n          Operation to load the repo at. By default, Jujutsu loads the repo at the most recent\r\n          operation, or at the merge of the divergent operations if any.\r\n          \r\n          You can use `--at-op=<operation ID>` to see what the repo looked like at an earlier\r\n          operation. For example `jj --at-op=<operation ID> st` will show you what `jj st` would\r\n          have shown you when the given operation had just finished. `--at-op=@` is pretty much the\r\n          same as the default except that divergent operations will never be merged.\r\n          \r\n          Use `jj op log` to find the operation ID you want. Any unambiguous prefix of the operation\r\n          ID is enough.\r\n          \r\n          When loading the repo at an earlier operation, the working copy will be ignored, as if\r\n          `--ignore-working-copy` had been specified.\r\n          \r\n          It is possible to run mutating commands when loading the repo at an earlier operation.\r\n          Doing that is equivalent to having run concurrent commands starting at the earlier\r\n          operation. There's rarely a reason to do that, but it is possible.\r\n          \r\n          [aliases: --at-op]\r\n\r\n      --debug\r\n          Enable debug logging\r\n\r\n      --color <WHEN>\r\n          When to colorize output\r\n          \r\n          [possible values: always, never, debug, auto]\r\n\r\n      --quiet\r\n          Silence non-primary command output\r\n          \r\n          For example, `jj file list` will still list files, but it won't tell you if the working\r\n          copy was snapshotted or if descendants were rebased.\r\n          \r\n          Warnings and errors will still be printed.\r\n\r\n      --no-pager\r\n          Disable the pager\r\n\r\n      --config <NAME=VALUE>\r\n          Additional configuration options (can be repeated)\r\n          \r\n          The name should be specified as TOML dotted keys. The value should be specified as a TOML\r\n          expression. This\r\ncan be avoided with `--no-edit`.\r\n\r\nNote that you can create a merge commit by specifying multiple revisions as argument. For example,\r\n`jj new @ main` will create a new commit with the working copy and the `main` bookmark as parents.\r\n\r\n[working copy]: https://docs.jj-vcs.dev/latest/working-copy/\r\n\r\nUsage: jj new [OPTIONS] [REVSETS]...\r\n\r\nArguments:\r\n  [REVSETS]...\r\n          Parent(s) of the new change [default: @] [aliases: -o, -r]\r\n\r\nOptions:\r\n  -m, --message <MESSAGE>\r\n          The change description to use\r\n\r\n      --no-edit\r\n          Do not edit the newly created change\r\n\r\n  -A, --insert-after <REVSETS>\r\n          Insert the new change after the given commit(s)\r\n          \r\n          Example: `jj new --insert-after A` creates a new change between `A` and\r\n          its children:\r\n          \r\n          ```text\r\n                          B   C\r\n                           \\ /\r\n              B   C   =>    @\r\n               \\ /          |\r\n                A           A\r\n          ```\r\n          \r\n          Specifying `--insert-after` multiple times will relocate all children of\r\n          the given commits.\r\n          \r\n          Example: `jj new --insert-after A --insert-after X` creates a change\r\n          with `A` and `X` as parents, and rebases all children on top of the new\r\n          change:\r\n          \r\n          ```text\r\n                          B   Y\r\n                           \\ /\r\n              B  Y    =>    @\r\n              |  |         / \\\r\n              A  X        A   X\r\n          ```\r\n          \r\n          [aliases: --after]\r\n\r\n  -B, --insert-before <REVSETS>\r\n          Insert the new change before the given commit(s)\r\n          \r\n          Example: `jj new --insert-before C` creates a new change between `C` and\r\n          its parents:\r\n          \r\n          ```text\r\n                             C\r\n                             |\r\n                C     =>     @\r\n               / \\          / \\\r\n              A   B        A   B\r\n          ```\r\n          \r\n          `--insert-after` and `--insert-before` can be combined.\r\n          \r\n          Example: `jj new --insert-after A --insert-before D`:\r\n          \r\n          ```text\r\n          \r\n              D            D\r\n              |           / \\\r\n              C          |   C\r\n              |    =>    @   |\r\n              B          |   B\r\n              |           \\ /\r\n              A            A\r\n          ```\r\n          \r\n          Similar to `--insert-after`, you can specify `--insert-before` multiple\r\n          times.\r\n          \r\n          [aliases: --before]\r\n\r\n  -h, --help\r\n          Print help (see a summary with '-h')\r\n\r\nGlobal Options:\r\n  -R, --repository <REPOSITORY>\r\n          Path to repository to operate on\r\n          \r\n          By default, Jujutsu searches for the closest .jj/ directory in an ancestor of the current\r\n          working directory.\r\n\r\n      --ignore-working-copy\r\n          Don't snapshot the working copy, and don't update it\r\n          \r\n          By default, Jujutsu snapshots the working copy at the beginning of every command. The\r\n          working copy is also updated at the end of the command, if the command modified the\r\n          working-copy commit (`@`). If you want to avoid snapshotting the working copy and instead\r\n          see a possibly stale working-copy commit, you can use `--ignore-working-copy`. This may be\r\n          useful e.g. in a command prompt, especially if you have another process that commits the\r\n          working copy.\r\n          \r\n          Loading the repository at a specific operation with `--at-operation` implies\r\n          `--ignore-working-copy`.\r\n\r\n      --ignore-immutable\r\n          Allow rewriting immutable commits\r\n          \r\n          By default, Jujutsu prevents rewriting commits in the configured set of immutable commits.\r\n          This option disables that check and lets you rewrite any commit but the root commit.\r\n          \r\n          This option only affects the check. It does not affect the `immutable_heads()` revset or\r\n          the `immutable` template keyword.\r\n\r\n      --at-operation <AT_OPERATION>\r\n          Operation to load the repo at\r\n          \r\n          Operation to load the repo at. By default, Jujutsu loads the repo at the most recent\r\n          operation, or at the merge of the divergent operations if any.\r\n          \r\n          You can use `--at-op=<operation ID>` to see what the repo looked like at an earlier\r\n          operation. For example `jj --at-op=<operation ID> st` will show you what `jj st` would\r\n          have shown you when the given operation had just finished. `--at-op=@` is pretty much the\r\n          same as the default except that divergent operations will never be merged.\r\n          \r\n          Use `jj op log` to find the operation ID you want. Any unambiguous prefix of the operation\r\n          ID is enough.\r\n          \r\n          When loading the repo at an earlier operation, the working copy will be ignored, as if\r\n          `--ignore-working-copy` had been specified.\r\n          \r\n          It is possible to run mutating commands when loading the repo at an earlier operation.\r\n          Doing that is equivalent to having run concurrent commands starting at the earlier\r\n          operation. There's rarely a reason to do that, but it is possible.\r\n          \r\n          [aliases: --at-op]\r\n\r\n      --debug\r\n          Enable debug logging\r\n\r\n      --color <WHEN>\r\n          When to colorize output\r\n          \r\n          [possible values: always, never, debug, auto]\r\n\r\n      --quiet\r\n          Silence non-primary command output\r\n          \r\n          For example, `jj file list` will still list files, but it won't tell you if the working\r\n          copy was snapshotted or if descendants were rebased.\r\n          \r\n          Warnings and errors will still be printed.\r\n\r\n      --no-pager\r\n          Disable the pager\r\n\r\n      --config <NAME=VALUE>\r\n          Additional configuration options (can be repeated)\r\n          \r\n          The name should be specified as TOML dotted keys. The value should be specified as a TOML\r\n          expression.
